skill.name: ac-nexus
skill.description: AC-Nexus helps AI agents control Broadlink and Xiaomi MIoT air conditioners through local network devices, weather rules, schedules, and storm shutdown logic.
skill.summary: Control supported air conditioners through Broadlink IR and Xiaomi MIoT workflows.
skill.author: oywq00008-cell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Most command and weak-crypto detections are Markdown documentation false positives, but the skill intentionally guides agents to install software, store API keys and device tokens, access local network devices, and run persistent AC schedules. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so publication is acceptable only with clear operational warnings.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Physical Device Automation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents that init creates durable state and starts a background scheduler that can continue AC on/off, auto-adjust, and storm shutdown behavior after the agent task ends. This can affect physical devices without later user interaction, so explicit consent and review are required.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that scheduled actions persist and continue autonomously. The behavior is intentional and safety-relevant because it controls physical air conditioners.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Device Token Persistence
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to store API keys, Xiaomi device tokens, and device configuration in a local hidden configuration directory. This is legitimate for the tool, but it creates sensitive local state that agents must not expose in logs or prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly references API keys, a token field, and persistent config storage. No malicious forwarding is shown, but the sensitive-data handling risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to External Services and Local Devices
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill requires network activity for cloning the project, querying MIoT specifications, using weather and storm providers, and communicating with local Broadlink or Xiaomi devices. These flows are expected for the skill but increase privacy and operational risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network endpoints and local device IP examples are present in the documented workflows. The context supports legitimate IoT control rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External Installation and Runtime Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells agents to clone an external repository, install requirements, and run Python control workflows. This is a normal setup path, but community-sourced dependency installation should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and Python examples are explicit, but the skill file itself is documentation rather than executable code. Risk depends on the external project and runtime environment.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby backtick execution inside a script. Some examples still describe real setup commands, which are covered separately as medium risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious characters appear in a Markdown file around tables and code examples. There is no Ruby file or shell script in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Detections
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static weak-cryptography hits occur in descriptive text and API tables. No evidence was found in SKILL.md of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic implementation code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows product descriptions and function documentation, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched substrings in prose or identifiers.

low_findings.title: Configuration Dictionary Reads Misclassified as Environment Access
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Lines reading current_brand_type and current_device_mac use a local config dictionary, not operating-system environment variables. This does not indicate secret environment access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code calls _cfg.config.get on application state. There is no os.environ or process.env style access in that snippet.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Directory Stores Operational State
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents hidden files under the user home directory for config, MIoT cache, and custom IR codes. This is expected, but it can retain API keys, device tokens, schedules, and learned device data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented paths use ~/.ac_controller and explicitly describe persisted config or learned codes. This is a concrete local data retention risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Scheduler Continues After Agent Task
dangerous_patterns.description: The scheduler can continue running after the initiating task completes and can execute scheduled AC changes or storm shutdown behavior. Users must approve schedule and automation changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The safety section and scheduling section both state that automation persists beyond the current process. The security concern is operational control, not hidden intent.

content.value_statement: Smart AC control often requires device-specific network commands and persistent schedules. AC-Nexus gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for Broadlink IR and Xiaomi MIoT air conditioner control.
actual_capabilities: Initializes AC controller configuration with location, brand, and weather provider settings.
Sends on, off, mode, temperature, and fan commands to supported Broadlink and Xiaomi MIoT devices.
Adds Xiaomi MIoT IR controllers after QR login and stores device metadata for local control.
Reads Xiaomi AC state through MIoT property requests when token and host data are available.
Uses weather and storm data to decide AC settings or shut down devices during storm risk.
Creates persistent schedule templates for multi-day AC automation.
limitations: Requires the external AC-Nexus Python project and its dependencies to be installed.
Needs valid device tokens, API keys, and local network access for most workflows.
Broadlink IR control is one-way unless the device supports separate state reporting.
Automation changes can persist after the agent task and must be reviewed by the user.

use_cases.title: Set Up Local AC Control
use_cases.description: Install the controller, initialize the config, and send a verified test command to one air conditioner.

use_cases.title: Manage Multi-Room Cooling
use_cases.description: List configured devices, match room names, and control a specific Broadlink or Xiaomi device.

use_cases.title: Automate Weather-Based Safety Actions
use_cases.description: Check weather or storm data and adjust AC behavior only after user approval.

prompt_templates.title: Check Setup Requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my environment for AC-Nexus setup. List required dependencies, credentials, devices, and safety confirmations before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Control One Device
prompt_templates.prompt: Use AC-Nexus to control the named air conditioner. Confirm the target room, power state, mode, temperature, and fan setting before sending the command.

prompt_templates.title: Add Xiaomi MIoT Controller
prompt_templates.prompt: Help add a Xiaomi MIoT IR controller to AC-Nexus. Guide QR login, list eligible devices, and explain what local token data will be stored.

prompt_templates.title: Design Safe Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an AC-Nexus schedule and weather automation plan. Show the proposed persistent changes and wait for approval before saving them.

output_examples.input: Turn on the living room AC at 25 degrees.
output_examples.output: Target device identified as living room. Proposed command: power on, cool mode, 25 degrees, auto fan. Awaiting confirmation before sending.

output: Explain that QR login is required.
Ask which controller to store before saving token and device details.

output_examples.output: Draft schedule prepared for weekdays with requested on and off times. This change is persistent and will not be saved until approved.

best_practices: Confirm the exact room and device identifier before sending any AC command.
Review every persistent schedule, token, and automation change with the user before saving.
Keep API keys and device tokens out of chat logs, screenshots, and shared reports.
anti_patterns: Do not enable schedules, auto-adjust, or storm shutdown without explicit user approval.
Do not paste Xiaomi tokens, API keys, or config files into public channels.
Do not run external installation commands without reviewing the source repository and dependencies.

faq.question: What devices does AC-Nexus target?
faq.answer: It targets Broadlink RM IR blasters and Xiaomi MIoT-compatible IR remote controllers used for air conditioner control.

faq.question: Does the skill require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses local network device control and may use external services for setup, weather, MIoT specifications, and releases.

faq.question: Does it store sensitive data?
faq.answer: Yes. Configuration can include API keys, device tokens, local IPs, schedules, and learned IR codes.

faq.question: Can automation keep running after the agent stops?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented scheduler can persist beyond the immediate agent task, so schedule changes need explicit approval.

faq.question: Is this safe for non-technical users?
faq.answer: It is best for technical users who understand local device control, credentials, and persistent automation risks.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The reviewed file shows legitimate IoT automation, but it involves credentials, local network control, external setup, and physical device actions.
